title: Resource management and serialization boundaries

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific source-linked draft lesson for Resource management and serialization boundaries; independent technical, source, pedagogy, accessibility, and runnable-example review remains required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: Learn Java; accessed 2026-07; Exceptions and Java I/O learning paths; atomic topic: Resource management and serialization boundaries
sources.claim: Try-with-resources closes each declared AutoCloseable in reverse order even when the body fails, and explicit interoperable data formats are safer boundaries than native object deserialization.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Try-with-resources closes each declared AutoCloseable in reverse order even when the body fails, and explicit interoperable data formats are safer boundaries than native object deserialization.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A BufferedReader and underlying stream are declared in try-with-resources so cleanup occurs on normal return and on parsing failure. Your goal is to implement a failure-safe resource workflow and explain its cleanup behavior. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Constructors and invariants; String and text processing; Paths, files and text I/O; Exception flow and stack traces. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: Try-with-resources closes each declared AutoCloseable in reverse order even when the body fails, and explicit interoperable data formats are safer boundaries than native object deserialization. This model is applied through the declared Java: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: Close itself can fail and become suppressed, ownership decides who closes a resource, some wrappers cascade closure, and untrusted serialized objects can execute dangerous construction paths. The learner must reject this documented misconception: Garbage collection promptly closes files and sockets, so explicit resource management only improves style.

includes: Use try-with-resources and prefer explicit data formats over unsafe native object deserialization.
Implement a failure-safe resource workflow and explain its cleanup behavior.
excludes: Close itself can fail and become suppressed, ownership decides who closes a resource, some wrappers cascade closure, and untrusted serialized objects can execute dangerous construction paths.

outcomes: Implement a failure-safe resource workflow and explain its cleanup behavior.

examples.title: Resource management and serialization boundaries: direct language case
examples.situation: A BufferedReader and underlying stream are declared in try-with-resources so cleanup occurs on normal return and on parsing failure.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Java: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: A BufferedReader and underlying stream are declared in try-with-resources so cleanup occurs on normal return and on parsing failure.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: Use try-with-resources for owned closeable resources and inspect primary plus suppressed failures; garbage collection is not a cleanup schedule.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because Use try-with-resources for owned closeable resources and inspect primary plus suppressed failures; garbage collection is not a cleanup schedule.

examples.title: Resource management and serialization boundaries: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: The application stores validated JSON fields rather than deserializing an arbitrary ObjectInputStream from an uploaded file.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: The application stores validated JSON fields rather than deserializing an arbitrary ObjectInputStream from an uploaded file.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: Close itself can fail and become suppressed, ownership decides who closes a resource, some wrappers cascade closure, and untrusted serialized objects can execute dangerous construction paths.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: Garbage collection promptly closes files and sockets, so explicit resource management only improves style.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Implement a failure-safe resource workflow and explain its cleanup behavior.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Garbage collection promptly closes files and sockets, so explicit resource management only improves style.
misconceptions.correction: Use try-with-resources for owned closeable resources and inspect primary plus suppressed failures; garbage collection is not a cleanup schedule.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Predict both examples and identify the first observable contradiction in this belief: Garbage collection promptly closes files and sockets, so explicit resource management only improves style.

analogy.example: a document desk that records a failure chain, handles only recoverable cases, and always closes borrowed equipment can introduce Resource management and serialization boundaries, after the canonical Java: Beginner to Intermediate rule is stated first.
usefulFor: exception boundaries, paths, text encodings, and resource cleanup
limitations: suppressed exceptions, stack unwinding, filesystem semantics, and serialization attacks need exact technical treatment
